political_offices.title: Governor
political_offices.notes: First Governor of Connecticut born within its limits. Longest-serving after Gov. Winthrop. Died in office Oct 11, 1741.

cross_connections.display_label: Son-in-law signed his estate — 'Nathaniel Hooker and Eunice Hooker'
His granddaughter Esther married Rev. Noadiah Russell in 1758
was the Deputy Governor whose death opened his path

notable.notable_blurb: First Connecticut governor born within its limits

narrative_blocks.header: First Connecticut governor born within the colony
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Hartford on November 16, 1669, Joseph Talcott was the first Governor of Connecticut born within the colony rather than in England. He served from 1724 until his death on October 11, 1741 — 17 years in office, longer than any predecessor except Governor Winthrop and any successor until the modern era. His funeral expenses of £69 12s. 6d. were paid from the Colonial Treasury.

narrative_blocks.header: He presided in the upper house the afternoon
narrative_blocks.body: When his wife Eunice died suddenly about noon on Thursday, May 25, 1738, the Deputy Governor was absent at Norwich. Governor Talcott did not let his private grief interfere with his official duty — he returned and presided in the upper house that same afternoon. The General Assembly appointed a committee to draw an address of condolence to the Governor.

narrative_blocks.header: His estate was signed by 'Nathaniel Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: At the Court of Probate, Hartford, on December 25, 1742, the distribution of Governor Talcott's estate was signed by all children as co-heirs. Among the signatures: 'Nathaniel Hooker and Eunice Hooker' — his daughter Eunice Talcott and her husband Nathaniel Hooker 2d. The Talcott-Hooker convergence was entered into the public record.

narrative_blocks.header: Three of his children each found a separate
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Eunice married Nathaniel Hooker 2d — direct Hooker surname. His son Matthew married Mary Russell, a Hooker descendant through the Pierpont branch. His daughter Abigail married Rev. Daniel Wadsworth; their son Jeremiah later married Mary's own sister Mehitable Russell, also a Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Petitioned for his father's estate by primogeniture
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Talcott was the fourth son of Lt.-Col. John Talcott Jr. but the eldest to survive his father. In 1691, at age 21, he petitioned the General Court for his father's estate by right of primogeniture — an assertive legal claim for colonial Connecticut. He was first mentioned in public life in 1711 as a committee member to lay out the town of Coventry.

notable.notable_blurb: First Connecticut governor born within its limits

Governor Talcott did not let his private grief interfere with his official duty — he returned and presided in the upper house that same afternoon. His daughter Abigail married Rev. Daniel Wadsworth; their son Jeremiah later married Mary's own sister Mehitable Russell, also a Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Talcott was the fourth son of Lt.-Col. John Talcott Jr. but the eldest to survive his father. In 1691, at age 21, he petitioned the General Court for his father's estate by right of primogeniture — an assertive legal claim for colonial Connecticut. He was first mentioned in public life in 1711 as a committee member to lay out the town of Coventry.

parents.father_research_notes: Ensign George Clark of Milford, Connecticut

bio.notes: Widow of [first husband Wakeman] before marrying Gov. Joseph Talcott (T00045)

narrative_blocks.header: Her four daughters never married; only the son
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Talcott, daughter of Connecticut governor Joseph Talcott, married the Reverend Daniel Wadsworth, Hartford's pastor and a member of the Yale Corporation. Of their six children, the four daughters who survived infancy all died unmarried; only her son Jeremiah, who wed Mehitable Russell, carried the family forward.

cross_connections.display_label: His mother's sister married into the Hooker family

narrative_blocks.header: His uncle hid Connecticut's Charter in the Oak
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Daniel Wadsworth's uncle Joseph Wadsworth was the man who seized the Connecticut Charter on the night of October 31, 1687 and hid it in the Charter Oak in Hartford — aided by Lt.-Col. John Talcott, the grandfather of Daniel's future wife Abigail Talcott. Daniel himself had no part in it; he was born 17 years later. But both families whose founding myth was the Charter Oak — Wadsworth and Talcott — converged in his household.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at 43
narrative_blocks.body: Daniel Wadsworth died on November 12, 1747, when his son Jeremiah was four years old. The boy was sent to be raised by his Talcott uncle Matthew Talcott of Middletown — his mother Abigail's brother. The man who would become Commissary General of the Continental Army and intimate friend of Washington grew up in a Talcott household, knowing his father's family only by reputation.

education.institution_blurb: Yale College 1726. Member of Yale Corporation 1743 until his death in 1747.

cross_connections.display_label: Edwards won his seat — then handed it back
Talcott cousins who both supplied Washington's army from CT
EH notes their Standley cousinship — before he married a Hooker
Raised him from age four after his father died
His grandmother Elizabeth Standley was sister to Abigail Standley Hooker.

notable.notable_blurb: Commissary General; Rochambeau's host; 2nd President Bank NY

narrative_blocks.header: Commissary General to both Washington and Rochambeau
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Commissary General of the Continental Army from April 1778, then assumed the same role for Rochambeau's entire French force when it arrived in July 1780. By 1781 he had marshaled 855 horses, 600 oxen, 239 wagon conductors, and bread ovens along the route from Newport to the Hudson — making the Yorktown campaign possible.

narrative_blocks.header: Washington and Rochambeau strategized in his Hartford parlor
narrative_blocks.body: On September 20-22, 1780, Washington and Rochambeau held their war strategy conference at his Hartford home, joined by Hamilton, Knox, and Lafayette. The conference ended abruptly when news of Benedict Arnold's treason at West Point reached them.

narrative_blocks.header: Raised by the uncle who became his brother-in-law
narrative_blocks.body: His uncle Matthew Talcott raised him from age four after his father died — and shipped him before the mast to improve his health. Years later he married Mehitable Russell, sister of Matthew Talcott's wife Mary, making his guardian also his brother-in-law.

narrative_blocks.header: Second president of the Bank of New York
narrative_blocks.body: He served as second President of the Bank of New York 1785-1786, working closely with his friend Alexander Hamilton on the early Republic's financial architecture. Edward Hooker called him 'one of the few men to whom the Revolutionary War proved a source of wealth.'

narrative_blocks.header: Father of the man who founded Wadsworth Atheneum
narrative_blocks.body: His son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum in Hartford — the oldest continuously operating public art museum in America. The museum opened in 1844, four decades after Jeremiah's death.

narrative_blocks.header: Edwards beat him then handed back the seat
narrative_blocks.body: In 1790 Pierpont Edwards — fellow Hartford figure and Hooker descendant — defeated him for the Connecticut Congressional seat. Edwards then declined to take it, forcing a special election that returned Wadsworth to Congress.

narrative_blocks.header: WWII Liberty ship named in his honor
narrative_blocks.body: During World War II the United States Liberty ship SS Jeremiah Wadsworth was named in his honor. It was a fitting echo of his Revolutionary commissary work — Liberty ships served as workhorses of America's wartime supply effort, just as he had supplied Washington and Rochambeau.

institutions.institution_blurb: His 1784 portrait by John Trumbull (ART030) hangs in the Atheneum his son Daniel founded.
Part of Hartford's elite network that financed the Connecticut Land Company.

education.institution_blurb: Honorary degrees from Yale and Dartmouth, 1796.

cross_connections.display_label: Sister-in-law — Mary Russell married her brother Matthew Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: She and brother Matthew both married Hooker descendants
narrative_blocks.body: She married Captain Nathaniel Hooker — a direct Hooker-surname descendant. Her brother Matthew Talcott simultaneously married Mary Russell, herself a Hooker descendant through the Pierpont line. Two of Governor Joseph Talcott's children marrying into the Hooker family in the same generation is why "Talcott" became a permanent name in the Hooker tree.

cross_connections.display_label: Both families joined through Talcott siblings.
His wife's brother Matthew also married into the Hooker family line.
Both tied to separate Talcott branches in the same family generation.
Both families joined through Talcott siblings a generation later.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Governor Talcott's daughter Eunice
narrative_blocks.body: Eunice Talcott was the second daughter of Joseph Talcott, Governor of Connecticut from 1725 to 1741. Their marriage directly joined the two founding Hartford families — reinforced when Eunice's brother Matthew simultaneously married Mary Russell, herself a Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Named co-heir in the Governor's estate
narrative_blocks.body: On December 25, 1742, Nathaniel and Eunice appeared before the Court of Probate in Hartford to sign Governor Joseph Talcott's estate distribution as co-heirs. It was one of the final official acts binding the Hooker family to the colonial governor's wealth.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale man, militia captain, Assembly representative
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale College in 1729 and held the rank of Captain in the Connecticut Militia as early as 1734. He served as Hartford's representative in the General Assembly across three sessions, while running a substantial Hartford mercantile estate.

narrative_blocks.header: Son James partnered in Hooker & Chaffee
narrative_blocks.body: His son James Hooker was a partner in the mercantile firm Hooker & Chaffee, which grew very successful during the Revolutionary War era. The firm continued the family's Hartford commercial standing into the next generation.

burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford, Hartford County, Connecticut. Cemetery linkage repair: existing person was listed in cemetery hooker_connections in prior pilots but burial.cemetery_id was null. Pilot104 closure of bidirectional reference.
Ancient Cemetery, New London, New London County, Connecticut.

cross_connections.display_label: His mother — daughter of Governor Joseph Talcott.
His maternal grandfather, Governor of Connecticut 1724-1741.
First cousin once removed via the Talcott family.
Wife of his cousin Wadsworth — also a Hooker descendant.
Father-in-law and partner in the Hooker and Chaffee firm.
his partner in the firm Hooker & Chaffee
brother of his wife Mary Chaffee

narrative_blocks.header: Hooker and Chaffee supplied the Continental Army
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker and Chaffee was one of New England's largest mercantile houses before the Revolution, transacting much business for the Colonial Government. The partners gave freely to the patriot cause and were seriously embarrassed at war's end.

narrative_blocks.header: His firm provisioned alongside Wadsworth's commissary
narrative_blocks.body: The Hooker and Chaffee firm operated alongside Jeremiah Wadsworth's larger commissary operation — both Hooker-Talcott descendants provisioning the same Continental Army. The Connecticut River Valley supply network was effectively a Hooker-tree internal collaboration.

narrative_blocks.header: His Windsor house is on the National Register
narrative_blocks.body: He built the James Hooker House ca. 1772 at 118 Palisado Avenue, Windsor — now part of the Palisado Avenue Historic District on the National Register of Historic Places. The interior was later gutted for Chaffee School classrooms but the Federal-period exterior survives under Greek Revival additions.

institutions.institution_blurb: Silent partner; one of New England's great Revolutionary-era trading houses.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: He built this house c.1772; it survives on Palisado Avenue's National Register district.

bio.title: Captain
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Captain James Hooker (1742-1805), Windsor CT merchant.

career.notes: Principal of INST070 Hooker and Chaffee. Succeeded to father's Hartford business with brothers; established Windsor branch. See INST070 for full firm history.

death.cause: Died Aug 27, 1763 — seven months after her father Nathaniel died Jan 27, 1763.

narrative_blocks.header: The firm's failure drove him from Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: As a partner in Hooker and Chaffee, he helped provision the Continental Army before French and Spanish spoliations brought the firm down after the war. He removed to Sackett's Harbor, New York, and later died at Herkimer; his wife Elizabeth survived him by just six months.

institutions.institution_blurb: Partner in the firm; dispersed to western New York after its failure.

parents.mother_research_notes: Eunice Howell (Wakeman) Talcott — second wife of Gov. Joseph Talcott.

sources.short: S. V. Talcott, Talcott Pedigree in England and America from 1558 to 1876

sources.notes: Authoritative biographical record covering Matthew Talcott's full Revolutionary War service

notable.notable_blurb: Colonel of Connecticut's 23rd Regiment in Revolution

cross_connections.display_label: He raised her future husband — after already marrying her sister
Raised him from age four — the guardian became his brother-in-law
Both Talcott siblings each married into separate Hooker family lines.
Childless himself, paid for his namesake nephew's Yale education.
His wife Mary Russell's mother, a Hooker granddaughter.

narrative_blocks.header: Sea captain, judge, militia colonel
narrative_blocks.body: Matthew Talcott was a sea captain, merchant, and ship owner out of Middletown. He served as judge of the Hartford County Court, colonel in the Connecticut militia, and as a selectman of Middletown. In 1756 he sat on a committee reviewing the circumstances of Cushoy, the elderly and impoverished Wangunk sachem, and made recommendations to the Connecticut General Assembly — one of the rare documented encounters between the Talcott family and indigenous Connecticut in the colonial record.

narrative_blocks.header: Commanded a regiment in the Revolution at 63
narrative_blocks.body: When the Revolution broke out, Matthew Talcott was already in his sixties. Gov. Jonathan Trumbull commissioned him Major of the 6th Regiment of Horse and Foot in November 1774; six months later, after Lexington and Concord, the General Assembly made him Colonel of the newly organized 23rd Regiment (Middletown and Chatham).

narrative_blocks.header: Named a nephew for him; paid his education
narrative_blocks.body: Matthew Talcott Russell, born 1761, was named by his father Rev. Noadiah Russell after Colonel Matthew Talcott — who had married his aunt Mary Russell and had no children of his own. Colonel Talcott met the expenses of his nephew's education. Matthew Talcott Russell went on to study law with Oliver Ellsworth, become State's Attorney for Middlesex County, and serve as a Yale tutor.

narrative_blocks.header: He raised his future sister-in-law's husband
narrative_blocks.body: When Rev. Daniel Wadsworth died in 1747, leaving four-year-old Jeremiah fatherless, Matthew Talcott took the boy in. Jeremiah grew up in Matthew's Middletown household calling him uncle. In 1764 Jeremiah married Mehitable Russell — the sister of Matthew's own wife Mary.

narrative_blocks.header: 1756: reviewed the case of Wangunk sachem Cushoy
narrative_blocks.body: As selectman of Middletown CT, Matthew Talcott served in 1756 on a committee that reviewed the circumstances of Cushoy — an elderly and impoverished Wangunk sachem — and made recommendations to the Connecticut General Assembly. The committee's role marked one of the formal colonial-era encounters between Middletown's town leadership and the Wangunk people whose ancestral lands the town occupied.

narrative_blocks.header: 1775: led the committee mining Middletown lead
narrative_blocks.body: The same May 1775 General Assembly that made Talcott colonel of the 23rd Regiment also placed him on a committee to procure lead for Connecticut's military stores. Middletown was the site of one of the few lead mines in colonial Connecticut, and the committee was authorized to refine the mine's ore, erect any furnace or building necessary, and draw on the State Treasurer for sums up to £400. The committee's work supplied bullet lead for Connecticut's regiments through the early years of the Revolution.

burial.plot_notes: Riverside Cemetery, Middletown, Middlesex County CT (per Findagrave).

career.notes: Sea captain and merchant out of Middletown CT. Owned several ships including the Sloops Hester, Dolphin, and Elizabeth.
Elected selectman of Middletown. In 1756 served on the committee that reviewed Cushoy's circumstances (see narrative_blocks).
Appointed Justice of the Peace in 1773.
Appointed Justice of the Quorum in 1776.
Per SV Talcott 1876: judge of the Hartford County Court.

military_service.notes: Nov 4, 1774: Gov. Jonathan Trumbull commissioned Talcott Major of the 6th Regiment of Horse and Foot. May 11, 1775: General Assembly appointed him Colonel of the newly organized 23rd Regiment (Middletown + Chatham). For most of its existence the 23rd remained in Connecticut as an administrative unit — monitoring the coast for British foraging parties, guarding public stores. Aug 1776: 23rd Regiment called to NY to reinforce Washington's Continental Army; Talcott wrote Aug 13, 1776 to the Pay Table requesting £500 advance. The regiment was stationed along the East River during the British landing at Kip's Bay (Sept 15, 1776) — resulted in precipitous American retreat. After serving through the New York crisis, Talcott (then 63) resigned his commission Oct 1776; replaced by Comfort Sage. Jun 9, 1777: still active in coastal defense, requested loan from State of three swivel guns + four dozen swivel-gun shot + wormer and ladle from New London (in care of Nathaniel Shaw Jr).

cross_connections.display_label: Sister-in-law — both joined the Hooker-Talcott nexus.
Sister-in-law — Eunice Talcott married a Hooker descendant.
her grandmother, first of the three Marys linked to the silk apron

burial.plot_notes: Riverside Cemetery, Middletown, Middlesex County CT (per Findagrave).

narrative_blocks.header: Married Talcott two months after mother's death
narrative_blocks.body: Her mother Mary Pierpont Russell died July 24, 1740. She married Matthew Talcott — son of Connecticut Governor Joseph Talcott — exactly two months later, on September 24, 1740 in Middletown.

narrative_blocks.header: Talcott double marriage with Eunice and Nathaniel
narrative_blocks.body: Her marriage to Matthew Talcott was paralleled by Matthew's sister Eunice marrying Capt. Nathaniel Hooker. The two Talcott siblings each married into the Hooker family in the same generation — one through Pierpont/Russell, one directly through the Hooker surname line.

narrative_blocks.header: Why 'Talcott' is a Hooker family middle name
narrative_blocks.body: This double Talcott-Hooker convergence — through her marriage and Eunice Talcott's — established the genealogical foundation for 'Talcott' as a middle name carried by Hooker descendants. Both lines descend from Hartford founders meeting in mid-eighteenth-century Middletown.

narrative_blocks.header: The last Mary, and the likeliest needle
narrative_blocks.body: Ending the direct maternal line with no heirs, Mary Russell Talcott is a leading candidate to have embroidered the family’s 1730s silk apron, since she would have attended finishing school in the right decade. The apron outlived the line that made it.

narrative_blocks.header: He bought the stones meant for a church
narrative_blocks.body: When Hartford's Episcopalians began their first church in 1762, the foundation stones ended up in Samuel Talcott's house instead. He had not led the mob that wrecked the work, but he bought the stones from a man with no right to sell them. The churchmen never forgave him for it.

narrative_blocks.header: “The gates of hell shall not prevail”
narrative_blocks.body: In 1786 the Episcopalians tried again, and at the laying of the cornerstone one of their leaders rose to speak. “I lay the corner-stone of the first Episcopal Church in Hartford,” he declared, “and Sam Talcott and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it.” His name had become a byword for the opposition.

narrative_blocks.header: His will named only three of his children
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel and Mabel raised a houseful of children, several of whom died young. When he made his will, he named only the three who outlived him: his son Samuel, his daughter Mary Watson, and his daughter Jerusha. The document quietly records how much a colonial family could lose.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of Connecticut Governor Joseph Talcott
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Talcott served as Colonial Governor of Connecticut from 1724 to 1741. She was born to him in Hartford in 1720, his fourth-year-in-office daughter.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband took the pulpit her nephew's father vacated
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Daniel Wadsworth had married her sister Abigail and pastored First Church Hartford until his death in 1747. Her husband Rev. Edward Dorr was ordained there the next year.

narrative_blocks.header: Childless across both her ministerial marriages
narrative_blocks.body: Neither Rev. Edward Dorr nor Rev. Robert Breck of Springfield gave her children. Her stepchildren came through Breck's first wife, who died in 1767 before he married her.

cross_connections.display_label: Married Gov. Talcott's granddaughter Esther Talcott in 1758

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1750 — entire ministry at one church
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale in 1750, was ordained at the First Church of Thompson Connecticut in 1757, and served there until his death in 1795. Thirty-eight years in one parish, his entire ministerial career.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Gov. Talcott's granddaughter — Hooker-Talcott convergence
narrative_blocks.body: He married Esther Talcott in 1758 — daughter of Joseph Talcott Jr., Treasurer of the Colony of Connecticut, and granddaughter of Gov. Joseph Talcott. This Russell-Talcott marriage echoed the convergence begun by his sister Mary Russell Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: Apoplexy at Rev. Caleb Alexander's house, Mendon
narrative_blocks.body: He traveled to Boston for his health in October 1795 but was seized with apoplexy en route. He died on the evening of October 27 at the house of Rev. Caleb Alexander in Mendon, Massachusetts.

narrative_blocks.header: His grandson co-founded Yale's Skull and Bones
narrative_blocks.body: His son Matthew Talcott Russell named a son William Huntington Russell, who at Yale in 1832 co-founded the secret senior society Skull and Bones with Alphonso Taft. The line connects this Thompson Connecticut pulpit to the most secretive of American institutions.

cross_connections.display_label: His childless uncle paid for his Yale education and gave his name.
His grandson carried the Talcott given name forward to a third generation.

narrative_blocks.header: His childless uncle paid for his Yale education
narrative_blocks.body: Col. Matthew Talcott had married his aunt Mary Russell and they had no children of their own. The Colonel met the expenses of his namesake nephew's schooling at Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Studied law under future Chief Justice Ellsworth
narrative_blocks.body: He read law in Windsor with Oliver Ellsworth, who would later draft the Constitution and serve as third Chief Justice of the United States. He was admitted to the Connecticut bar in January 1786.

narrative_blocks.header: His son co-founded Skull and Bones at Yale
narrative_blocks.body: His fifth son William Huntington Russell graduated Yale in 1833. William had co-founded the Skull and Bones secret society there in 1832 as an undergraduate.

narrative_blocks.header: Sixteen years as Middletown City Treasurer
narrative_blocks.body: He held the office continuously while serving as State's Attorney for Middlesex County. He was ordained Deacon of the church in April 1798.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale tutor before settling into law
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Yale tutor from November 1782 until May 1786. He was admitted to the College Church in 1785 during this period.

cross_connections.display_label: Sea chest left her line for the Larned line in 1788.
Family donated the William Russell sea chest in 1888.

narrative_blocks.header: 1888 letter may have confused her with Abigail
narrative_blocks.body: The 1888 William Russell sea chest provenance letter attributes the chest to her marriage to Thaddeus Larned. Genealogical records suggest the letter may have confused her with her sister Abigail Russell Larned, who actually married Thaddeus.

narrative_blocks.header: Sea chest left Russell line via Larned marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Her marriage to Thaddeus Larned in the 1780s carried the 1638 William Russell Sea Chest out of the direct Russell line. The chest passed through three Larned generations before her great-grandnephew George Huntington Larned donated it back to the New Haven Colony Historical Society on the 250th anniversary of its 1638 arrival.

narrative_blocks.header: Carried William Russell sea chest into Larned marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Russell took the William Russell Sea Chest with her on marrying Thaddeus Larned in the 1780s — the family heirloom passing into the Larned line through her marriage. Later genealogical accounts misattributed the transfer to 'Esther Russell,' but documentation through her son George Larned (born 1792) establishes that Abigail, not Esther, was the bride who carried the chest forward. The chest remains a tracked Hooker-line artifact today.

narrative_blocks.header: Died age thirty at Thompson, Connecticut, 1797
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Russell Larned died March 7, 1797 in Thompson Connecticut at age thirty — six years after her son George Larned's 1792 birth and ten years into her marriage. The short life at age thirty was typical of late-eighteenth-century childbearing-age mortality. She is buried at West Thompson Cemetery in Thompson Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Thompson CT 1766 to 1797; one documented son
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Russell was born July 20, 1766 in Thompson Connecticut and died March 7, 1797 there at age thirty. Her one documented child was George Larned (born 1792). She married Thaddeus Larned (born 1756, died 1818) — Thaddeus outlived her by twenty-one years.

education.notes: — Yale 1793. Pastor, Princeton MA; later mercantile Troy NY; retired Milford CT.

political_offices.title: Deputy / Assistant Magistrate
political_offices.notes: Elected before Connecticut joined New Haven Colony

political_offices.title: Treasurer
political_offices.notes: Succeeded his father May 17, 1660; held office until 1676

political_offices.title: Major, then Lieut.-Colonel
political_offices.notes: Resigned Treasurer and took command of Connecticut army at outbreak of King Philip's War

notable.notable_blurb: Colonial military commander, King Philip's War

narrative_blocks.header: He was named guardian of the charter
narrative_blocks.body: When Connecticut's royal charter arrived from Charles the Second in 1662, the freemen chose Talcott, with Samuel Wyllys and Lieutenant John Allyn, as the committee sworn to keep it in their custody. A quarter-century later he would help spirit that same charter away from Andros.

narrative_blocks.header: He took the charter as the candles died
narrative_blocks.body: By Roger Wolcott's 1764 account, when the candles were snuffed in the Hartford meeting-house in 1687, Talcott and Nathaniel Stanley lifted the two colonial charters from the table before Andros could seize them. Wolcott's telling names no oak; the tree entered the tradition later, and the duplicate passed to his son-in-law Joseph Wadsworth.

narrative_blocks.header: Connecticut's victorious commander in King Philip's War
narrative_blocks.body: Talcott led Connecticut's forces through King Philip's War in 1675 and 1676, winning a string of engagements that earned him promotion to lieutenant-colonel. His campaigns ranged from the river towns down into the Narragansett country.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept the colony's purse for sixteen years
narrative_blocks.body: Talcott served as treasurer of Connecticut Colony from 1660 to 1676, taking the office directly from his father before him. The family's account books became a running ledger of early Hartford.

narrative_blocks.header: He died intestate, his estate worth thousands
narrative_blocks.body: Talcott died in 1688 without a will, leaving an estate appraised at more than two thousand pounds, a substantial colonial fortune. It passed by primogeniture to his eldest surviving son, the future governor Joseph Talcott.

battles: Multiple engagements with Wampanoag and allied tribes
military_service.notes: Led Connecticut standing army with 200 Mohegan and Pequot Indian allies. 'In the various battles with the Indians in which he was engaged, he was always victorious, and obtained great renown as an Indian fighter.' Promoted from Major to Lieut.-Colonel during the war.

parents.notes: Her father John Wakeman was Colony Treasurer — the same office her husband John Talcott Jr. would later hold
parents.father_research_notes: John Wakeman, Treasurer of the Colony of Connecticut

open_questions.question: Full Talcott genealogy entry
open_questions.notes: Compiler has Talcott genealogy book. Children and descendants to be entered as T-prefix entries.

burial.plot_notes: Buried in Hartford. Name inscribed on monument erected by citizens of Hartford to perpetuate memory of the founders of the Colony of Connecticut.

political_offices.title: Select Man (Selectman)
political_offices.notes: Chosen at general town meeting Feb 4, 1634 along with Haynes, Bradstreet, and four others

political_offices.title: Representative
political_offices.notes: One of Representatives for Newtown (Cambridge), May 14, 1634

emigration.notes: 123 passengers including 50 children; all arrived in good health after 12-week passage. Fellow passengers included William Wadsworth, Tobie Willet (possible relation to Mary Willet who married Rev. Samuel Hooker), James Olmstedd, and others of Hooker's Company.

hartford_land.notes: Nine parcels originally granted: dwelling house on east side of Main Street abutting Centinel Hill; seven acres east of Great River; five acres in North Meadow; forty-seven acres meadow/swamp in North Meadow; one acre in Little Meadow; thirty acres in Old Oxpasture; seventeen acres in Cow Pasture; eleven acres in Venturers Field; sixteen acres in Neck of Land. Also gained 25 additional parcels by acquisition.

will.bequests_summary: Dwelling house and lands to wife Dorothy for her natural life; then to son John. Wethersfield property to son Samuel. Grandchildren John Russell (£20), John Talcott, Samuel Talcott, Elizabeth Talcott (£5 each). £5 to Rev. Samuel Stone; £5 toward a Latin school at Hartford. Overseers: Richard Lord Sr. and son John.
will.gift_to_stone: Five pounds to 'my reverend and beloved teacher, Mr. Stone' — Rev. Samuel Stone, Hartford minister and colleague of Thomas Hooker

co-founded Hartford with Talcott
Both in the Adventurers Party 1635; both Hartford founders.
His son Samuel's marriage brought Pynchon-Holyoke blood into the Talcott line.
was his grandson, the long-serving Barnstable minister

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founder of Hartford, Connecticut alongside Rev. Thomas Ho…
narrative_blocks.body: Co-founder of Hartford, Connecticut alongside Rev. Thomas Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: The only Talcott known to have emigrated
narrative_blocks.body: The only Talcott known to have emigrated to America — all American Talcotts descend from him

narrative_blocks.header: Sailed on ship Lyon June 22
narrative_blocks.body: Sailed on ship Lyon June 22, 1632 with Hooker's Company, arriving Boston Sept 16, 1632

narrative_blocks.header: Among the 'Adventurers Party' of 25 men who
narrative_blocks.body: Among the 'Adventurers Party' of 25 men who explored the Hartford area October 1635, prior to Hooker's party departure May 1636

narrative_blocks.header: Fifth greatest proprietor of houses and lands
narrative_blocks.body: Fifth greatest proprietor of houses and lands in Newtown (Cambridge) 1634

narrative_blocks.header: His carpenter Nicholas Clark built the first house
narrative_blocks.body: His carpenter Nicholas Clark built the first house in Hartford on the ground where the North Church (Dr. Bushnell's) later stood

narrative_blocks.header: Was styled 'The Worshipful Mr. John Talcott'
narrative_blocks.body: Was styled 'The Worshipful Mr. John Talcott' in colonial records

narrative_blocks.header: Mother's daughter Rachel married Hartford Founder John S…
narrative_blocks.body: His mother's daughter Rachel married Hartford Founder John Steele; her sister Sarah married Hartford Founder William Wadsworth; he was an uncle of Hartford Founder John Skinner

bio.notes: The only Talcott known to have emigrated to America. Co-founder of Hartford with Rev. Thomas Hooker.

parents.discrepancy_note: One source gives parents as 'John Mott and Alice Harington'; Talcott 1876 gives 'Mark Mott and Frances Gutter' with Mark being son of Thomas and Alice (Mead) Mott. The Talcott 1876 reconstruction preferred as more detailed.
parents.mother_research_notes: Probably Frances Gutter (wife of Mark Mott). Frances died Braintree, buried Feb 23, 1615. Mark Mott died Braintree, buried Dec 14, 1637.
parents.father_research_notes: Probably Mark Mott, son of Thomas and Alice (Mead) Mott of Braintree, Essex. Talcott 1876 says 'supposed to have been the daughter of Mark, son of Thomas and Alice (Mead) Mott.'

burialCemetery.name: Ancient Burying Ground
burialCemetery.notes: Adjacent to Center Church. Earliest surviving identifiable gravestone dates to 1648. Resting place of many Hartford founders including Rev. Thomas Hooker (exact location unknown).

crossConnections.display_label: Son-in-law signed his estate — 'Nathaniel Hooker and Eunice Hooker'
His granddaughter Esther married Rev. Noadiah Russell in 1758
was the Deputy Governor whose death opened his path
